ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for spiced dry-fried chickpea dish; closest defensible anchor is 'chickpea food product' (FOODON_03315671).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked chickpeas; no dedicated code for South Indian dry-spiced preparation. Closest parent match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared legume dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side dish serving of Chana Dry is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a generous scoop served alongside rice or flatbread at an Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Chickpeas (Bengal gram, cooked) with adaptation for spiced dry preparation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN values for cooked Bengal gram (chickpeas) used as the primary nutritional anchor. Values adjusted upward for sodium (225 mg/100g) and fat (6 g/100g) to account for added salt and cooking oil in the spiced dry preparation. Energy, protein, carbohydrate, and fibre values are consistent with ICMR-NIN cooked chickpea data. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chickpeas, cooked, boiled, without salt; FDC ID 173756) used as secondary cross-reference to validate macro ranges.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Chickpeas, boiled (GI = 28)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 28 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Chana Dry is prepared from boiled chickpeas dry-fried with spices; the spice coating and minimal added fat do not materially alter the GI of the chickpea base. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (28 × 38.0) / 100 = 10.6, using 200 g serving with 19 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 225 mg/100g — borderline; a 200 g serving contributes 450 mg sodium, approximately 20% of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced salt during preparation to keep sodium below 200 mg/100g, avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums, and rely on the dish's high dietary fibre (5 g/100g) and estimated potassium (~320 mg/100g) to support blood pressure management consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 28) with 5 g/100g dietary fibre and 7.5 g/100g protein, which together slow glucose absorption and blunt post-meal glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chana Dry is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI of 28 and high fibre content; a standard 200 g serving contributes approximately 38 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~10.6, which is within a manageable range when paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than refined white rice or white bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1 g/100g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, and high dietary fibre (5 g/100g) support a heart-healthy nutritional profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chana Dry is appropriate for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and 5 g/100g dietary fibre which supports LDL cholesterol reduction; sodium at 225 mg/100g should be monitored and preparation salt reduced where possible to align with the AHA recommendation of less than 1500 mg sodium per day for those with CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, garlic) and high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome immune regulation; low saturated fat.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chana Dry may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile; however, those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor symptom response as the dish may contain tomatoes and green chilli, both of which are nightshade vegetables.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chana Dry (also known as Sukka Chana or Sukha Chana) is a savoury Indian legume side dish made from boiled chickpeas (Bengal gram) that are dry-fried and tossed with a fragrant blend of South Indian spices including curry leaves, mustard seeds, cumin, turmeric, coriander, green chilli, garlic, ginger, and onion. The dish is cooked until the chickpeas are well-coated and the moisture has evaporated, resulting in a dry, slightly crispy exterior with a tender interior. The flavour profile is earthy, mildly spicy, and aromatic, with the toasted curry leaves and mustard seeds providing a characteristic South Indian character. In Singapore, Chana Dry is commonly served as a side dish at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls, often accompanying rice, roti prata, or biryani. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 160 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 6 g fat (of which only 1 g is saturated), 19 g carbohydrate, 5 g dietary fibre, and 225 mg sodium. The dish has a low glycemic index of 28, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Its high fibre content supports digestive health and cardiovascular protection. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. The dish is a good source of plant-based protein and iron, and the anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, ginger, garlic) adds functional health value beyond basic nutrition.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Chana Dry?
Is Chana Dry good for diabetics?
Is Chana Dry high in protein?
Can I eat Chana Dry if I have high blood pressure?
meal_context: Served with roti prata or chapati

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked chickpea data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. No exact published nutritional analysis for this specific Singaporean restaurant preparation of Chana Dry was found in approved sources. Values are plausible and internally consistent but represent an adapted estimate rather than a direct laboratory measurement of the dish as served.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this spiced chickpea preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central at the dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this dry-cooked preparation is not reported in available approved sources. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data at the prepared dish level is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): chickpeas and vegetable cooking oil contain negligible trans fats; confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked chickpeas. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): chickpeas are a plant-based food containing no cholesterol; confirmed per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central. potassium_mg of 320 mg/100g is an estimate based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy value for cooked chickpeas (~291 mg/100g) adjusted upward slightly for the spice and aromatics contribution. GI of 28 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008 for boiled chickpeas; applied as analogous estimate for this dry-spiced preparation. Sodium at 225 mg/100g is borderline; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt preparation.
